coredata.dc:title: Zero-energy states in graphene quantum dot with wedge disclination
coredata.dc:creator: Bouhlal, Ahmed
Jellal, Ahmed
Shah, Nurisya Mohd
coredata.dc:description: We show that wedge disclination affects the density of states for fermions that are electrostatically confined in graphene quantum dots. By considering a magnetic flux and solving the Dirac equation, we determine the eigenspinors. These are used for large arguments to derive an approximate scattering matrix, which allows us to get the density of states. Under various conditions, it is found that the density of states exhibits several resonance peaks. In particular, it is shown that the wedge disclination is able to change the amplitude, width, and positions of resonance peaks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Quantum dot
Electrosatic potential
Magnetic flux
Wedge disclination
Density of states

head.ce:title: Zero-energy states in graphene quantum dot with wedge disclination

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Theoretical Physics, Faculty of Sciences, Chouaïb Doukkali University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We show that wedge disclination affects the density of states for fermions that are electrostatically confined in graphene quantum dots. By considering a magnetic flux and solving the Dirac equation, we determine the eigenspinors. These are used for large arguments to derive an approximate scattering matrix, which allows us to get the density of states. Under various conditions, it is found that the density of states exhibits several resonance peaks. In particular, it is shown that the wedge disclination is able to change the amplitude, width, and positions of resonance peaks.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Graphene is a special carbon material that forms a honeycomb network with conduction and valence bands touching each other at two asymmetric points
( K ,
K
′
)
, called Dirac points [1–3]. Because of its unique electronic, optical, and mechanical properties [4–6], graphene has attracted a lot of attention since its first experimental isolation in 2004. Indeed, the energy dispersion is linear near
( K ,
K
′
)
, and the charge carriers have a minimal conductivity
4
e
2
/ π h
[1,7,8]. Graphene is a good thermal and electrical conductor, as well as being transparent and flexible. The quantum Hall effect in graphene may also help to achieve even more precise resistances in metrology [9]. It is a material notably characterized by high surface area, superior mechanical flexibility, and high electron mobility
1
0
6
cm
2
V−1 s−1 [10]. It has been discovered that charge carriers in graphene can be completely transmitted through an electrostatic barrier, a phenomenon known as Klein tunneling [7,11,12].

The absence of the band gap in graphene presents an obstacle to its technological applications. There are several methods for opening a band gap in graphene, such as cutting it into nanoribbons [13]. Another approach, known as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), is based on reducing the dimension from 2D to 1D graphene nanoribbon and 0D graphene flakes. Moreover, the isolation of a GQD offers the possibility to generate a band gap [14–16]. Because quantum dots have properties similar to atoms, they are sometimes referred to as artificial atoms [17]. When compared to other semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs have excellent properties such as high stability, low toxicity, tunable fluorescence, high quantum efficiency, and chemical inertness [18,19]. Quantum dots have been shown to be very promising candidates for future quantum information techniques [14,20]. Indeed, the tunable electronic structure of quantum dots has been used in photodetection [21,22], photovoltaics [23,24], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [25,26], and plasmonics [27,28].

For zero magnetic flux, the Berry phase quantifies the kinematic angular momentum to half-integer values. This excludes the presence of a zero angular momentum state that corresponds to a normal incidence in Klein tunneling. As a result, all states of a quantum dot are confined to some degree [29]. Because the presence of flux causes the kinematic angular momentum to take integer values, states that cannot be confined by the electrostatic potential alone can appear [30]. Some integrable geometries, such as the disk [31,32], can be used to get states that exclude normal incidence. This disk can be realized by confining fermions in a circular region of graphene, surrounded by an undoped sheet of graphene. It was shown that a disk-shaped quantum dot is an ideal 2D system with linear energy dispersion and a density of states that cancels at zero energy [33].

We study the density of states (DOS) for a system based on graphene having a wedge disclination obtained through the construction of Volterra [34]. For this, we consider a circular GQD of radius R surrounded by a circular ring of undoped graphene of radius L bound by a metallic contact with a wedge disclination
n = 0 , ± 1 , ± 2
and in the presence of a magnetic flux Φ and electrostatic potential. We use the asymptotic behavior of Hankel functions for large arguments to determine the scattering matrix and therefore derive DOS.

Subsequently, we numerically analyze the DOS under various conditions for the physical parameters. This allows us to show different oscillatory behaviors and find resonances. We show that the peaks of DOS strongly depend on the angular momentum quantum number m . During the scanning of the gate voltage, the zero-energy bound states cause resonant peaks that become narrow as long as L increases. Furthermore, we discover that n acts by changing the resonance peaks. This behavior contrasts strongly with that of
n = 2
(square defect), which moves to the right from its initial position where
n = 0
(no defect), and then the resonance becomes more and more acute. In the opposite case,
n = − 2
(octagon defect), we observe the creation of a new peak that corresponds to
m = 5 / 2
.

The paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we establish a theoretical model describing electrostatically confined charge carriers in geometry obtained by Volterra construction. We compute the scattering matrix by restricting ourselves to the asymptotic behavior of Hankel functions solution the energy spectrum in Section 3. This matrix will allow us to explicitly determine the associated DOS in Section 4. In Section 5, we present a numerical analysis of the DOS under various conditions and show that several resonance peaks exist. Finally, we conclude our results.

The configuration we study consists of a GQD connected to a ring-shaped metal contact, and the two are separated by an undoped intrinsic graphene layer, as depicted in Fig. 1. The Volterra construction is used to introduce the wedge disclination by removing a sector of
δ = n π / 3
[34] . The integer n represents the type of disclination in the honeycomb lattice, with
n = − 2
representing an isolated octagon defect,
n = − 1
representing an isolated heptagon defect,
n = 0
representing an isolated defect in the no defect case,
n = 1
representing an isolated pentagon defect, and
n = 2
representing an isolated square defect.

The Hamiltonian of a graphene monolayer that describes the motion of a single electron through an electrostatic potential
V
( r )
at low energy is
(1)
H =
v
F
τ

) denote the sublattice and valley degrees of freedom in the basis of the two sublattices of A and B atoms, respectively.

We recall that for

, the Bessel functions can be approximated by

), we have

Now for

and

, we find that the results of (31) remain valid for all

.

We will determine the density of states (DOS) at zero energy in terms of the potential
V
( r )
using the scattering matrix
S
μ
( ϵ )
for low energy (28). The local DOS
ρ
( r , ϵ )
is given by the derivative of
S
μ
( ϵ )
[41–43]

.
We calculate the total DOS of the circular quantum dot by integrating

( r , ϵ )

.

Taking into account the continuity of the eigenspinors (17) and (19) at
r = R
for zero-energy, we establish the resonance condition
(37)

. At resonance, one can write

.
For zero-energy, we show that the DOS has a Lorentzian variation on

. Indeed, for zero flux (

where the resonance width is given by

.
Next, we numerically analyze the obtained DOS under suitable conditions and emphasize the role of the wedge disclination n .

DOS as a function of
V
0
R
is shown in Fig. 2 for the wedge disclination index
n = 0 , ± 1 , ± 2
, and valley
τ
z
= ± 1
. Recall that each value of n represents a different type of defect with (a): no defect
n = 0
, (b,d): pentagon
( 1 , ± 1 )
, (e,f): square
( 2 , ± 1 )
, (g,h): heptagon
( − 1 , ± 1 )
, (i,j): octagon
( − 2 , ± 1 )
. We observe that the DOS presents resonance peaks with oscillatory behavior that are marked by their angular momentum m . The position of the resonances, as well as the scale of amplitude and width, are all in good agreement with the results obtained in [30,44–46] for
n = 0
. By varying n , we see that the positions, amplitude, and width change. Positive values of n and valley K (
τ
z
= 1
) shift the peaks to the right, which is due to the peak suppression. For
n = 2
and
τ = 1
, we observe that only four peaks remain with increasing amplitudes and decreasing width, as shown in Fig. 2e. For negative n and
τ
z
= 1
, the resonance peaks change their initial positions and move to the left, which explains the appearance of three peaks corresponding to
m = 5 / 2
,
m = 11 / 2
and
m = 13 / 2
for
n = − 2
. The amplitudes of these peaks decrease as the width increases. Now, for the valley
K
′
(
τ
z
= − 1
), we notice that the effect of wedge disclination n is reversed. Indeed, for n negative, the resonance peaks move to the right with an increase in amplitude and a decrease in width. The condition (37) shows that there are three resonance peaks labeled according to their angular momentum
m = 1 / 2
. However, they are missing in two cases:
( n = 2 , τ = − 1 )
and
( n = − 2 , τ = 1 )
, as shown in Fig. 2(f,i).

The characteristics of the resonance are found to be dependent on the value of n and the valley
τ
z
, as shown in 3. Indeed, the resonance positions shift to the right if
τ
z
n > 0
and become very important for
n = 2
and
τ
z
= 1
, whereas they shift to the left for
τ
z
n < 0
. When
n > 0
, the influence of this magnitude where
n > 0
on the behavior of the resonance peaks is reversed. For
τ
z
= − 1
, we observe that n has no effect on the position or characteristics of the second resonance peak, as clearly seen in Fig. 3d. We notice that the peaks of the second resonance are very narrow compared to those of the first one. As n increases in Fig. 3c, the resonance peaks become very narrow, such that their amplitude increases but their width decreases.

In Fig. 4, we only consider the first resonance and investigate its influence on the magnitude of the point-lead coupling
R / L
, where L denotes the distance between the source and the drain. We observe that the resonance peaks have Lorentzian shapes whose properties depend on several parameters, including n and ratio
R / L
. For
τ
z
= 1
and
n = 2
(square), the resonance peaks become narrower when the quantum dot is very far from the source, i.e., when
R / L = 0
. The width of the resonances is independent of the coupling to the source and drain, which is determined by
R / L
, and their height increases when
R / L
is small.

The width of the resonance peak Γ as a function of wedge disclination n is shown in Fig. 5 for
m = 1 / 2 , 3 / 2 , 5 / 2 , 7 / 2
, and
τ
z
= ± 1
. For high values of m , the resonances become very sharp when
n = 2
and
τ = 1
, which is consistent with the width scale (37). Figs. 5b and 3d show that n does not affect the characteristics and position of the second resonance peak for
τ
z
= − 1
. Furthermore, the variation of the amplitude of resonance peaks is inversely related to the variation of their width. Consequently, these numerical values confirm the results obtained above.

We now analyze the effect of magnetic flux
Φ
i
= 1 / 2
that yields an integer total angular momentum μ labeling the resonance peaks. Indeed, in Fig. 6, we plot DOS as a function of
V
0
R
, with
R / L = 0 . 2
for
n = 0 , ± 1 , ± 2
, and
τ
z
= ± 1
. Here, the electrons collect an Aharonov–Bohm phase π , which cancels the effect of the Berry phase. Then
Φ
i
= 1 / 2
generates a state with zero angular momentum
μ = 0
for the system, which cannot be confined by an electrostatic potential. For
n = 0
(no defect), we find results in terms of the position and scale of the width by changing the resonances
R / L
that are consistent with previous simulations [30,44–46]. Furthermore, we observe very narrow resonance peaks.

DOS of the first and second resonances
μ = 1 , 2
is represented in Fig. 7 for
τ
z
= ± 1
with
n = 0
(blue line),
n = 1
(green line),
n = 2
(magenta line),
n = − 1
(purple line), and
n = − 2
(orange line). We observe that the effect n becomes very important for
n = 2
and
τ
z
= 1
with very narrow resonance peaks well shifted to the right, leading to a suppression of four peaks. However, for
n = − 2
and
τ
z
= 1
, we notice a decrease in amplitude of the peaks and appearance of three peaks, indicating that the number of bound states increases.

We investigate the effect of the ratio
R / L
on DOS for first resonance
μ = 1
for
Φ
i
= 1 / 2
,
τ
z
= ± 1
and varying n . By comparing Figs. 4 and 8, we show that the analysis can be made quantitative by taking a specific peak and studying the effect of
R / L
. We observe that DOS becomes very saturated when the limit
R / L → 0
is reached, which corresponds to a weak coupling between the quantum dot and a ring-shaped metallic. It is clearly seen that the behavior of resonance peaks is affected by n ,
Φ
i
, and
R / L
. To give a better presentation of the result, we plot another curve representing the width of the resonance peaks.

The width of the resonance peak for
μ = 1 , 2 , 3 , 4
is plotted in Fig. 9 for two values of
τ
z
= ± 1
. Comparing Figs. 5 and 9, we observe that the effect of wedge disclination n becomes very important for the valley
K
(
τ
z
= 1 )
. Also, we notice that the effect of n enjoys the presence of a magnetic flux.

The amplitude of resonance peak as a function of magnetic flux
Φ
i
for the angular momentum
m =
1
2
and
n = 1 , 2
is shown in Fig. 10. We observe that the amplitude grows exponentially with
Φ
i
and the variation becomes very important for
n = 2
and
τ
z
= 1
, which is in agreement with that obtained in [46,47].

We studied the density of states (DOS) of a circular graphene quantum with a wedge disclination
n = 0 , ± 1 , ± 2
subjected to a magnetic flux Φ . We found that DOS at zero energy shows several peaks that are associated with new localized states, which was verified by calculating the local density of states. Changes are observed in the available quasi-bound states due to the disclination n and new peaks in DOS. These results showed that there are more quasi-bound states in the structure with the defect n . They include edge states caused by the circular boundaries of the finite lattice and the defect, states resulting from circular confinement, and states of atoms that belong to the defect.

Most resonances have a width that disappears more rapidly as both L , distance from the center of the quantum dot to the metal contacts, and n increase, indicating well-confined states. Our results show that n acts as a source of spin flux of opposite sign for the two spin components when n is positive and contrary for negative n
[39,48]. For heptagon defect
( n = − 1 )
and octagon defect
( n = − 2 )
, we found that DOS is constituted of a series of peaks such that their number increases but their amplitude decreases, which is responsible for weak confinement. Contrary to the pentagon defect
( n = 1 )
and square defect
( n = 2 )
, we have shown the opposite of the previous findings, knowing that the number of bound states is decreasing but confinement is becoming very important. The importance of confinement becomes much more pronounced when a flux tube carrying half-quantum flux is applied to the system. In summary, we showed that the topological defect, which produces an effective gauge field coupled to the electrons of graphene, can modify the physical properties of the system.

Ahmed Bouhlal: Contributed to the design and implementation of the research, To the analysis of the results, To the writing of the manuscript. Ahmed Jellal: Contributed to the design and implementation of the research, To the analysis of the results, To the writing of the manuscript. Nurisya Mohd Shah: Contributed to the design and implementation of the research, To the analysis of the results, To the writing of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is no conflict of interest to declare.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electric field effect in atomically thin carbon films

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. S. Novoselov, A. K. Geim, S. V. Morozov, D.-e. Jiang, Y. Zhang, S. V. Dubonos, I. V. Grigorieva, and A. A. Firsov, Electric field effect in atomically thin carbon films, science 306, 666 (2004)
A. Neto, Ah castro neto, f. guinea, nmr peres, ks novoselov, and ak geim, Rev. Mod. Phys 81, 109 (2009)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrathin epitaxial graphite: 2D electron gas properties and a route toward graphene-based nanoelectronics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Berger, Z. Song, T. Li, X. Li, A. Y. Ogbazghi, R. Feng, Z. Dai, A. N. Marchenkov, E. H. Conrad, P. N. First, et al., Ultrathin epitaxial graphite: 2d electron gas properties and a route toward graphene-based nanoelectronics, The Journal of Physical Chemistry B 108, 19912 (2004)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intrinsic and extrinsic performance limits of graphene devices on SiO2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.-H. Chen, C. Jang, S. Xiao, M. Ishigami, and M. S. Fuhrer, Intrinsic and extrinsic performance limits of graphene devices on sio2, Nature nanotechnology 3, 206 (2008)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoscience and Technology: A Collection of Reviews from Nature Journals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. K. Geim and K. S. Novoselov, The rise of graphene, in Nanoscience and technology: a collection of reviews from nature journals ( World Scientific, 2010) pp. 11–19

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of individual gas molecules adsorbed on graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Schedin, A. K. Geim, S. V. Morozov, E. W. Hill, P. Blake, M. I. Katsnelson, and K. S. Novoselov, Detection of individual gas molecules adsorbed on graphene, Nature materials 6, 652 (2007)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chiral tunnelling and the Klein paradox in graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Katsnelson, K. Novoselov, and A. Geim, Chiral tunnelling and the klein paradox in graphene, Nature physics 2, 620 (2006)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electronic properties of disordered two-dimensional carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Peres, F. Guinea, and A. C. Neto, Electronic properties of disordered two-dimensional carbon, Physical Review B 73, 125411 (2006)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Towards a quantum resistance standard based on epitaxial graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Tzalenchuk, S. Lara-Avila, A. Kalaboukhov, S. Paolillo, M. Syväjärvi, R. Yakimova, O. Kazakova, T. Janssen, V. Fal’Ko, and S. Kubatkin, Towards a quantum resistance standard based on epitaxial graphene, Nature nanotechnology 5, 186 (2010)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Theoretical predictions of size-dependent carrier mobility and polarity in graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.-Q. Long, L. Tang, D. Wang, L. Wang, and Z. Shuai, Theoretical predictions of size-dependent carrier mobility and polarity in graphene, Journal of the American Chemical Society 131, 17728 (2009)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum interference and Klein tunnelling in graphene heterojunctions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. F. Young and P. Kim, Quantum interference and klein tunnelling in graphene heterojunctions, Nature Physics 5, 222 (2009)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Die reflexion von elektronen an einem potentialsprung nach der relativistischen dynamik von Dirac

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Klein, Die reflexion von elektronen an einem potentialsprung nach der relativistischen dynamik von dirac, Zeitschrift für Physik 53, 157 (1929)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Energy band-gap engineering of graphene nanoribbons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Y. Han, B. Özyilmaz, Y. Zhang, and P. Kim, Energy band-gap engineering of graphene nanoribbons, Physical review letters 98, 206805 (2007)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chaotic Dirac billiard in graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. A. Ponomarenko, F. Schedin, M. I. Katsnelson, R. Yang, E. W. Hill, K. S. Novoselov, and A. K. Geim, Chaotic dirac billiard in graphene quantum dots, Science 320, 356 (2008)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electron-hole crossover in graphene quantum dots

H. Edwards, Q. d. Niu, and A. De Lozanne, A quantum-dot refrigerator, Applied physics letters 63, 1815 (1993)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Calvo, Electrostatic quantum dots in a suspended graphene monolayer, Physical Review B 84, 235413 (2011)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Pal, W. Apel, and L. Schweitzer, Electric transport through circular graphene quantum dots: Presence of disorder, Physical Review B 84, 075446 (2011)
A. C. Neto, F. Guinea, N. M. Peres, K. S. Novoselov, and A. K. Geim, The electronic properties of graphene, Reviews of modern physics 81, 109 (2009)

Y. V. Nazarov, Y. Nazarov, and Y. M. Blanter, Quantum transport: introduction to nanoscience (Cambridge university press, 2009)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Friedel sum rule for a system of interacting electrons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Langer and V. Ambegaokar, Friedel sum rule for a system of interacting electrons, Physical Review 121, 1090 (1961)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Capacitance, admittance, and rectification properties of small conductors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Buttiker, Capacitance, admittance, and rectification properties of small conductors, Journal of Physics: Condensed Matter 5, 9361 (1993)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current partition in multiprobe conductors in the presence of slowly oscillating external potentials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Büttiker, H. Thomas, and A. Prêtre, Current partition in multiprobe conductors in the presence of slowly oscillating external potentials, Zeitschrift für Physik B Condensed Matter 94, 133 (1994)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Density of states as a probe of electrostatic confinement in graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Schneider and P. W. Brouwer, Density of states as a probe of electrostatic confinement in graphene, Physical Review B 89, 205437 (2014)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Charge transport in graphene with resonant scatterers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Titov, P. Ostrovsky, I. Gornyi, A. Schuessler, and A. Mirlin, Charge transport in graphene with resonant scatterers, Physical review letters 104, 076802 (2010)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Density of states analysis of electrostatic confinement in gapped graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Bouhlal, A. Jellal, and H. Bahlouli, Density of states analysis of electrostatic confinement in gapped graphene, Solid State Communications 333, 114335 (2021)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mass-profile quantum dots in graphene and artificial periodic structures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Gutiérrez-Rubio and T. Stauber, Mass-profile quantum dots in graphene and artificial periodic structures, Physical Review B 91, 165415 (2015)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.-L. Qi and S.-C. Zhang, Spin-charge separation in the quantum spin hall state, Physical review letters 101,
